items.title: The Feel-Good Story of the World Cup Is Too Good to Be True
items.description: Every World Cup propels a breakout star into the firmament; this year’s might just be a seemingly random German soccer fan who goes by Freddy. In the World Cup’s opening week, his X posts extolling a Taco Bell as “the holy land” and chronicling his rapturous 1 a.m. visits to a Waffle House and a Buc-ee’s have attracted more attention—from Americans, at least—than most of the actual matches. Freddy from Germany is the standard-bearer of an emergent social-media genre: A World Cup visitor from overseas encounters American culture and excess—and loves it. The Spanish soccer wunderkind Lamine Yamal loaded up a grocery cart at a Walmart in Georgia . “Why did no one tell me ranch sauce is like crack?” a Swedish fan posted on X from an Indiana diner. “EUROPE WE NEED RANCH ASAP.” A Japanese man raved about Texas Roadhouse steak. Freddy’s Buc-ee’s post showed customers flowing into the cavernous convenience store, its cartoon-beaver logo a towering beacon that illuminated the night sky. In another photo, a row of pumps stretched, like a horizon, beyond both sides of the frame. Freddy was overawed: “DUDE LMAO THIS IS A GAS STATION😭😭😭,” he wrote. Americans, of course, are eating it up with a spork. “This is genuinely making me patriotic,” one wrote of a video showing a rotund New Jersey–deli guy dancing with a visitor from London and giving him a chicken-parm sandwich on the house. Another observed : “It’s sick to see how many Europeans came over here to actually enjoy US culture. Saw a guy look at a Buc-ee’s gas station the same way I’d look at Stonehenge.” The caption on a video of an Italian’s astonished reaction to unlimited soda refills captured the half-winking exceptionalism in a familiar meme: “The European mind cannot comprehend this.” The videos have been covered in the media as a refreshing antidote to our polarized political moment and as an indication that American greatness resides at least partly in conveniences we take for granted. It’s a nice thought. But not all of the videos, or the people behind them, are quite what they seem. Take the Swedish soccer fan who swooned over ranch dressing. Elsa Thora, a photogenic 24-year-old blonde, has been featured in a number of news stories about foreign soccer fans’ American exploits, exuding a gee-whiz gusto for the country’s food and culture. “I feel like I’m in a movie,” she posted , holding bags of Hostess Twinkies and cheese-stuffed Combos outside a convenience store. “OK so Amish people are real,” she marveled . What many of the news stories have failed to mention is that Thora is not new to the social-media spotlight. She’s a star on the adult platform OnlyFans and a fixture in the British tabloids, where she’s made headlines for expressing her desire to have sex in space, to birth Elon Musk’s first baby on Mars, and to sleep with a player from every English Premier League soccer club. (“Three down, 17 to go,” she told The Irish Sun in 2024.) She already has some 388,000 followers on Instagram. When I reached her by phone, on her way to Los Angeles, she told me that she works in digital marketing but that the trip to the States was just for fun and the love of soccer. She acknowledged that her posts here have raised her social-media profile but insisted that she isn’t trying to monetize them: Her enthusiasm for American culture is genuine, she said. Thora may have more to gain from gushing than an ordinary soccer fan, but at least she’s a real person who’s really here for the World Cup. The same cannot be said for Nobunaga, a user whose viral X post purports to recount a Japanese visitor’s experiences at an American hibachi restaurant. “I witnessed a ritual I have never seen in eight hundred years of being Japanese,” begins a deadpan story that goes on to describe the chef building a flaming volcano of onion rings and hurling a shrimp through the air for the narrator to catch with his mouth. The post is part of a series in which Nobunaga takes on the persona of a samurai traveling around the world and into space. (The account shares a name with Oda Nobunaga, a powerful, 16th-century samurai.) Running the posts through Pangram , an AI-detection tool, yields a consistent verdict of “100% AI.” Nobunaga told me via X message that the aim of the stories is comedy, not “realism, journalism, or persuasion.” But at least in the context of the World Cup, some of the account’s audience seems to have taken its American-restaurant reviews at face value. “I am enjoying your posts with enthusiasm and a few tears,” a woman, whose X bio identifies her as a Trump supporter and patriot, replied to the hibachi post. Between the influencers and the AI-slop accounts lies a spectrum of inauthenticity. The New Jersey–deli guy, for instance, is real, but the Parkwood Deli’s 400,000-follower Instagram account suggests that the shop is canny about marketing his old-school charm. And in fact, the deli first posted the clip of his interaction with the London couple on Instagram in April, which suggests it had nothing to do with the World Cup, despite its current popularity. (Parkwood Deli did not respond to my request for comment.) Also real is the Japanese man who praised Texas Roadhouse for its steak. The account’s owner, who told me his name is Keisuke Yamanaka, really does enjoy Texas Roadhouse, and he isn’t a professional influencer. But he also isn’t in the United States for the World Cup, and the image he posted wasn’t his own, he acknowledged. “To be completely honest, when I posted the tweet, I never imagined it would reach millions of people,” Yamanaka said. “I simply wanted to share a restaurant recommendation with Japanese fans traveling to the United States for the World Cup.” Then there’s Freddy. A month ago, he had an unremarkable social-media presence, posting almost exclusively in support of the legendary Portuguese forward Cristiano Ronaldo. An April post on X about his upcoming trip to the World Cup in the United States got a modest 60 likes. Then he landed in Atlanta, his starting point for a road trip to Houston with his unnamed companions, and his profile achieved liftoff. He snapped those pictures of the Taco Bell and got 48,000 likes. On June 8, he bestowed a 10/10 rating on his Waffle House visit—109,000 likes. And then, on June 10, the Buc-ee’s blockbuster: 305,000 likes, more than 25 million views. Suddenly Freddy was a rock star. He started taking requests for places to visit next. He hit up a Bass Pro Shops that he said had a shooting range inside it. By June 12, he was fending off fake Freddies who had sprung up on Instagram and TikTok to try to cash in on his newfound fame. In New Orleans, he was given free tours of the Saints and Pelicans facilities. A sign on the highway at the Louisiana border reads Welcome to Louisiana ; below it was a hand-painted banner: Welcome Freddy . In Houston, Freddy hype took on a life of its own. The police department posed with him for a photo op. The mayor met him at an Astros game. The American-football star J. J. Watt bought him a lavish hotel stay and a care package full of swag. By Tuesday, he was at NASA’s Johnson Space Center getting a personal tour of the Orion capsule from the astronaut Anne McClain. Freddy did not take a time-out from the ride of his life to respond to my request for comment. Posts like Freddy’s and Thora’s tell a story that’s flattering to Americans—that unfettered consumerism has gotten a bad rap; that, contrary to conventional wisdom, there is such a thing as American culture; that it deserves celebration, even adoration. But telling people what they want to hear is exactly what today’s attention economy is designed to do. Algorithms don’t guarantee that those posts will reflect reality. As long as the demand for the content is genuine, the supply doesn’t have to be. No doubt at least some of the mutual enthusiasm between Americans and visiting football fans is sincere. Bostonians joined Scotland’s “Tartan Army” as it marched through their city, blaring bagpipes and drinking its pubs dry. Philadelphians embraced a crowd of raucous Ecuadorans who draped their national team’s yellow jersey over the city’s beloved Rocky statue (a classic blunder , as it turns out). It’s probably not a coincidence that so much American hospitality has been showered on Freddy, the character whose story feels the closest to authentic of the bunch. I verified with spokespeople for both the Houston police and mayor’s office that his interactions with them really happened. Yet even Freddy is keeping close control of his own story and has studiously stayed anonymous, at least to online fans. He has been careful not to show his face in any of his posts and has not disclosed his real name. Whatever his initial intentions for posting through his World Cup trip, his great American adventure has gathered a momentum that professional influencers dream of. When Freddy found the care package from Watt in his hotel room, he was moved. “This is all so insane... I genuinely don’t understand how it got to this point,” he posted . “We’re just normal World Cup tourists.😭😭” If that was ever true, it isn’t anymore.

items.title: Cour d’appel de Conakry : une dame poursuivie par le fils de son défunt mari pour des faits de stellionat
items.description: Les réquisitions et plaidoiries dans l’affaire opposant le parquet général à Aminata se sont déroulées ce mercredi 17 juin 2026 devant la cour d’appel de Conakry. La prévenue, qui comparaît libre, est poursuivie pour des faits de stellionat à la requête de Mamadou, fils de son défunt mari. Ce dernier l’accuse d’avoir vendu un bien immobilier présenté comme appartenant à son époux décédé, alors qu’il serait en réalité sa propriété. Les faits remontent au mois de mars 2026, à Sonfonia. En première instance, devant le tribunal correctionnel de Dixinn, Aminata a été déclarée coupable. Selon le parquet général, représenté par le procureur Ousmane Sankhon, la prévenue a été condamnée en première instance à trois ans d’emprisonnement, dont un an assorti de sursis, pour avoir vendu un bien appartenant à l’un des fils de son défunt mari. Le magistrat a également rappelé qu’elle avait bénéficié d’une mise en liberté provisoire à la demande de son avocat. Poursuivant ses réquisitions, l’avocat général a souligné que les faits avaient été reconnus à la barre par la prévenue. Il a ainsi requis sa condamnation à deux ans d’emprisonnement. Prenant la parole pour son dernier mot, Aminata a déclaré : « J’ai déjà commencé à rembourser. Je vous prie de me pardonner afin que je puisse payer le reste de l’argent. » À l’issue des débats, le président de la cour a mis l’affaire en délibéré pour une décision qui sera rendue le 8 juillet prochain. Elisa CAMARA L’article Cour d’appel de Conakry : une dame poursuivie par le fils de son défunt mari pour des faits de stellionat est apparu en premier sur Mediaguinee.com .

items.title: 50 ans plus tard, l'Afrique du Sud se rappelle les émeutes de Soweto

À l'époque, des étudiants noirs ont été réprimés par la police de Johannesburg dans un terrible massacre. Au moins 176 personnes sont mortes sous leurs balles. Récit de Lou Kisiela.

items.title: Un émissaire du Président mauritanien chez le Capitaine Ibrahim Traoré
items.description: (Ouagadougou, 17 juin 2026). Le Président du Faso, Chef de l’État, le Camarade Capitaine Ibrahim TRAORÉ, a accordé, ce mercredi dans la matinée, une audience au Ministre de la Défense, des Affaires des retraités et des enfants de martyrs de la Mauritanie, Hanana OULD SIDI. Le Ministre mauritanien était porteur d’un message du Président Mohamed Ould Cheikh El GHAZOUANI à son homologue burkinabè. Dans son message, le Président de la République islamique de Mauritanie adresse «ses vœux sincères de paix, de stabilité, de prospérité et de progrès pour le Peuple burkinabè», souligne le visiteur à sa sortie d’audience. Selon l’émissaire mauritanien, l’entretien avec le Chef de l’État, le Capitaine Ibrahim TRAORÉ, a été également une occasion de saluer l’excellence des relations d’amitié, de coopération et de solidarité qui unissent les deux pays. Les deux pays ont réaffirmé leur volonté de renforcer davantage les échanges et les concertations sur les questions d’intérêt commun portant sur la souveraineté, la sécurité, l’intégration régionale et le développement. Direction de la communication de la Présidence du Faso L’article Un émissaire du Président mauritanien chez le Capitaine Ibrahim Traoré est apparu en premier sur Wakat Séra .

items.title: L’armée ukrainienne peut-elle couper la Crimée de la Russie ?
items.description: La campagne ukrainienne de frappes de drones à longue et moyenne portée pose un défi considérable à la Russie, qui peine à intercepter les projectiles en nombre suffisant. C’est certainement en Crimée, la péninsule ukrainienne illégalement annexée par Moscou en 2014, que ces difficultés sont les plus visibles. Depuis plusieurs semaines, Kiev mène une campagne de ciblage quasi systématique des routes et voies logistiques qui relient le territoire russe à la Crimée. Sur le réseau Telegram, les photos et vidéos montrant des camions détruits par des drones le long des routes du Sud de l'Ukraine, sous occupation russe, se multiplient. Selon le commandant des Forces de systèmes sans pilote de l'armée ukrainienne, Robert Brovdi, le trafic sur l'autoroute de Novorossia (« Nouvelle Russie »), surnommée ces derniers mois « l’autoroute de la mort » par les forces ukrainiennes, se serait effondré de plus de 60 % en un mois Ukraine's drone commander wants to cut Crimea off from Russia », Reuters , 11 juin 2026.'> 2 . Cette campagne est rendue possible par le développement considérable de la production ukrainienne de drones , qui reflète les investissements majeurs réalisés dans son industrie domestique. Kiev produit désormais plusieurs centaines de milliers d’engins sans pilote chaque mois, et peut monter en quelques heures des opérations impliquant des dizaines de drones moyenne distance (de 100 à 300 kilomètres environ). En comparaison, les opérations qui reposaient auparavant sur des missiles occidentaux, comme des Storm Shadow/ SCALP ou des HIMARS, pouvaient quant à elles prendre plusieurs jours voire plusieurs semaines « Відтепер вважайте його островом ». Хто і як проводить операцію « відрізання » Криму », BBC , 13 juin 2026.'> 3 . La campagne ukrainienne a d’ores et déjà un impact majeur sur la péninsule. Dès le 6 juin, le « gouverneur » russe de Sébastopol, Mikhaïl Razvojaïev, a annoncé la mise en place d’un système de distribution du carburant, limitant les retraits à 20 litres par personne par semaine. Des restrictions avaient déjà été mises en place fin mai en raison des frappes ukrainiennes sur les voies logistiques et les raffineries, mais la détérioration de la situation depuis a conduit le ministère de l’Énergie à annoncer le 8 juin la création d’un État-major dédié à « assurer la stabilité » des approvisionnements en carburant dans les « régions du Sud » Publication sur Telegram du ministère russe de l’Énergie , 8 juin 2026.'> 4 . Les pénuries de carburant dans la péninsule affectent durement le secteur touristique, avec des taux d’occupation des hôtels deux fois inférieurs aux moyennes saisonnières ”Аннулируется 80 % заездов”. Из-за бензинового кризиса россияне стали массово отказываться от отдыха в Крыму », The Moscow Times , 15 juin 2026.'> 5 . Kiev revendique ouvertement chercher à « couper la Crimée de la Russie » en ciblant les approvisionnements. Cette stratégie, si elle est pour l’heure efficace, est néanmoins difficile à accomplir en raison de la capacité théorique de transport de biens et de marchandises depuis la Russie vers la péninsule. Les capacités générales de fret (via le pont de Kertch, les ferries, le corridor terrestre et les ports de Crimée) se situent entre 75 000 et 150 000 tonnes par jour, soit 2 à 4 fois plus que les besoins civils des habitants de la péninsule (40 000 tonnes) The Crimean Logistics Strikes and the Limits of Blockade Claims », Delwin – Military Theorist, 17 juin 2026.'> 6 . Plutôt que de chercher à rendre impossible tout approvisionnement, ce qui nécessiterait des investissements considérables, l’armée ukrainienne vise à contraindre Moscou à mobiliser des effectifs supplémentaires pour défendre les voies logistiques. Les frappes de drones en Crimée et dans le Sud de l’Ukraine ont également un impact direct sur l’approvisionnement des forces russes, ce qui contribue au ralentissement de la progression de l’armée sur toute la longueur du front. L’article L’armée ukrainienne peut-elle couper la Crimée de la Russie ? est apparu en premier sur Le Grand Continent .

items.title: «CFEE dans le centre»: l’IEF de Mbacké rassure sur l’organisation
items.description: Le Certificat de fin d’études élémentaires a démarré ce mercredi 17 juin dans le département de Mbacké, comme dans le reste du pays. Une tournée des autorités, conduite par l’adjoint au préfet et l’inspecteur de l’Éducation et de la Formation (IEF) , Abdoulaye Oumar Kane, a permis de constater le bon déroulement des épreuves dans plusieurs centres. Pour cette session 2026, Mbacké enregistre 10 418 candidats dont 7 045 filles et 3 373 garçons, répartis dans 46 centres d’examen. Touba-Mosquée concentre 25 de ces centres. L’innovation majeure de cette année est la publication des résultats dans les 10 jours qui suivent les épreuves. Les corrections et la proclamation se feront directement dans chaque centre. Pour réussir ce pari, toutes les dispositions ont été prises en amont, selon l’IEF, Abdoulaye Oumar Kane. Il a expliqué que les enseignants correcteurs convoqués seront présents dès le vendredi 19 juin, les corrections débuteront le samedi 20 juin, puis suivront la saisie et la vérification des notes. L’IEF assure que le personnel a été formé à cette nouvelle procédure déjà testée lors de l’essai national. Les insuffisances relevées à l’époque ont été corrigées, ce qui rend l’IEF confiant quant à la réussite de la réforme. Les partenaires de l’école, comme le SELS, saluent les conditions d’organisation et estiment que les élèves sont bien pris en charge. L’autorité administrative confirme que les mesures arrêtées lors de la réunion préparatoire sont respectées sur le terrain. Birane Diop, correspondant

items.title: The art of the swarm: Systemic rivalry with China on European terms
items.description: Summary Europe’s leaders are clear-eyed about the threat China poses to the continent’s industry, political economy and security. But to date, they have responded one tariff, one sector and one security restriction at a time. These measures show Beijing exactly where to apply pressure to deter an effective response from Europe. Instead, the EU needs to turn its system into its power. It should adopt six conditions for single-market access built on transparency, fair competition and reciprocity. In the years it takes the EU to build these instruments, the bloc should use the many tools it already has in a “swarm”. Each measure alone would be too small to warrant retaliation from Beijing. But together they would defend the single market and reduce critical dependencies, thereby creating space for negotiation. If China did retaliate, the EU would gain the political justification and the legal flexibility to take stronger, more lasting measures. In short, Europe’s era of acquiescence is over. Europe’s time to say no Thirty years ago, a group of Chinese nationalist authors wrote a bestseller, “China can say no”, a polemic urging the country’s leadership to refuse subordination to a US-led order. Beijing soon curtailed its circulation, but the book’s spirit runs through Xi Jinping’s focus on national strength, autonomy and self-reliance. China has had remarkable success in persuading European policymakers that this is not an answer they can give, too. Even as the case for saying no grows, Beijing’s economic policies are fuelling Europe’s deindustrialisation. The continent’s supply-chain dependencies on China leave it exposed to coercive pressure and debilitating shocks, and China’s support for Russia magnifies Europe’s biggest security threat. Yet according to Beijing, every move to protect the functioning of the free market is protectionism; China’s industrial domination is inevitable; and retaliation is a prospect so horrible that Europe cannot afford to act. The real problem, the story goes, is that Europeans just need to work harder and deregulate. So, Europe’s policymakers have serially deferred action while the continent loses industrial capacity, its dependencies deepen and its vulnerabilities worsen. Some of this is inaction is the product of Chinese campaigns. Much of it, however, reflects a set of stories Europe has been telling itself, which veered from complacency to fatalism at remarkable speed. China’s threats have reinforced this caution. Unnerved by Beijing’s success in facing down the Trump administration’s tariff escalation, European leaders have been wary of even considering steps that might spiral out of control. Where Europe has responded, it has been endemically reactive; by the time the policy is moving in the right direction, the facts on the ground have moved in the wrong direction faster. Recently, however, it has become all too clear that this cannot continue. With entire sectors being hollowed out and Beijing’s plans to dominate the technological transition advancing, yet more inaction would lock Europe into an accelerated decline. Policymakers will go into the June 2026 European Council meeting with a new sense of urgency. When Europeans finally do say no, they need to say it right. This paper makes the case for how: by turning Europe’s system—its regulatory standards, market rules, transparency requirements and coalition structures—into a source of strategic power, underpinned by the weight of the single market. The target is not China’s behaviour, but the market logic that makes that behaviour profitable. The approach rests on six conditions of access—the Six Nos—that translate Europe’s existing principles into a coherent strategic framework. They are intended to guide how European policymakers design and deploy instruments, and how they frame and defend the EU’s strategy at home and to Beijing. This paper lays out the case for these six conditions and explains how Europeans should implement them and scale them with like-minded partners. The paper concludes with how these conditions can also survive potential Chinese retaliation. These principles are not new nor are they China-specific. They exclude no company by its origin, only by an unwillingness to meet the terms that Europe insists on for access to its own market. For a transparent, market-based, secure supplier they are a modest cost of doing business in the world’s largest market; for a firm that depends on opacity, subsidy or state direction, they are a high one. As a result, they are as much an offer to Europe’s partners as a constraint on Beijing. Applied consistently, they can become a systematic advantage for those that meet them, giving them faster access, preference in procurement and eligibility for support. The aim is to alter the structure of the market so that transparency, diversification and competitive neutrality become the rational choices for firms seeking access to Europe. The EU’s most effective new instruments, however, remain years from full implementation, and there is no time to waste. So the paper also proposes implementing these principles through a swarm of emergency measures. These would use many of the EU’s existing instruments simultaneously and at intensity—from safeguards to subsidy investigations—rather than any single high-visibility measure, such as a large across-the-board tariff, that gives Beijing an easy focal point for retaliation. European policymakers should nonetheless assume that China will test the strategy with coercive pressure. If it does, the EU should see this as an opportunity to respond with even stronger measures, helping build negotiating space and resetting the relationship on firmer terms. This framework will inevitably raise costs in some sectors, expose exporters to Chinese retaliation and require support for industries whose competitiveness cannot be restored overnight. While these trade-offs cannot be eliminated, they are more bearable if they are anticipated, shared and tied to a credible strategy. Much of what is called the “cost” of resilience, however, is something else: a risk that the market has been failing to price. Europe has been paying for its dependencies already with firms getting the benefits of concentration while the public absorbed the risk. Building that risk in now is a correction of that mispricing, and set against it, the costs of inaction are higher still Withstanding the pressure and articulating the case for these trade-offs will depend on European leaders’ political will. This will be easier to generate and maintain when they believe the measures will work, the principles are worth defending and the framework is resilient—and when the approach is rooted in the comparative advantages of the European system itself. The reckoning European industry is being hit by the strengths and weaknesses of the Chinese economy at the same time. State-backed firms with vast scale, falling costs and accelerating technological sophistication are tough competition, while stagnant Chinese demand, deflation and chronic overcapacity push its output into Europe. In the first quarter of 2026, China recorded its largest-ever trade surplus with the EU—$83bn, on $148bn of exports against $65bn of imports, according to Beijing’s own figures—with the bloc now accounting for some 31% of China’s entire goods-trade surplus. But these figures still understate the impact: at deflated prices, the volume of Chinese imports has grown even faster. Europe’s industrial core is feeling the pressure. The French planning agency has assessed that a quarter of French exports, one-third of German exports and two-thirds of German domestic production are directly threatened by Chinese competitors, with “entire segments of European industry” liable to collapse within a few years. The Rhodium Group consultancy estimates that half of all German manufacturing jobs are now at risk, and that the EU is losing 500 manufacturing jobs a day to inadequate defences against Chinese competition. [1] Europe’s industrial difficulties are certainly not all China’s doing. Also weighing heavily are high energy costs, weak post-pandemic demand, monetary tightening, fragmented capital markets and regulatory burdens. Some of these things are self-inflicted, some cyclical. But until recently China barely featured in the European competitiveness debate, and when it did, it was only one trade irritant among many. Influential companies complained about regulations, climate targets and energy prices all while staying silent on China. At times, they actively lobbied against basic security, trade-defence and industrial-policy measures. This fostered the illusion, especially in Germany , that if Europe simply deregulate d, slowed its electrification ambitions, signed a few more trade deals, improved its product offers and strengthened European access to the Chinese market, the rest would take care of itself. The effects are as much political as they are economic. The “first China shock” of the 2000s, when cheap Chinese goods overwhelmed Western markets, did lasting political damage: the speed and geographic concentration of the economic hit led to a measurable growth in political extremism in the affected regions, and far-right parties in particular. The same pattern is visible in Germany’s industrial districts. The damage is difficult to attribute. It is easier for populists to blame the government, immigration or the energy transition than a competitor they never see, so the political costs are rarely traced to their cause. Where the first shock hit lower down the value chain, this one is rippling through the higher-value production of Europe’s industrial core. This time, these regions cannot move up the value chain to escape it. China is targeting precisely the sectors—advanced automation, robotics, new materials—that would have been the next phase of Europe’s industrial evolution. The continent stands to lose entire industrial ecosystems on a scale that has made observers reach for new analogies: more a steamroller than a shock. In a vicious cycle, this deindustrialisation is deepening Europe’s dependency. China accounts for around 30% of world manufacturing and is projected to reach 45% by 2030, with monopoly or near-monopoly positions across critical-minerals processing, permanent magnets, solar, batteries, drones and key pharmaceutical inputs. Europe’s reliance on these reaches as far as defence, where rearmament depends on Chinese products, including magnets and critical minerals. Beijing has already begun to restrict these exports, leaving European defence capacity hostage to the decisions of Moscow’s strategic partner . Meanwhile, by supporting Russia’s war economy, Beijing is enabling the very threat Europe is rearming against. This support has mainly consisted of dual-use supply: the components, machine tools, microelectronics and industrial inputs that have allowed Russia to sustain military production under sanctions. But since mid-2025, the relationship is extending into military-industrial cooperation on the capabilities likely to matter most in a future conflict with Europe—including drones and electronic warfare . Thanks to China, Europe’s ability to sustain a prolonged confrontation on robust industrial foundations is weakening just as the capacity sustaining its main adversary grows. Finally, these same dependencies create latent vulnerabilities for Europe. Connected systems —including grid inverters and transformers, legacy Huawei and ZTE equipment, COSCO-linked port concessions and connected vehicles—mean Beijing can disrupt or hold at risk the systems on which daily life depends, especially where remote access, software updates, telemetry and cloud dependence are involved. Some member states have begun to treat these as wartime vulnerabilities and are moving to rip out high-risk suppliers. Others, however, remain on a peacetime footing, with plans to phase out such suppliers distant or non-existent. Beijing no longer even pretends to be willing to negotiate seriously over these trends. At any suggestion that Europe might defend its industries or reduce its vulnerabilities, it is dismissive at best and threatening at worst. After years of European warnings about the economic imbalance and the cost of backing Russia went unmatched by serious action, Beijing has learnt that Europe may complain, but it can be coerced into delay. Covenants without the sword Facing such a reckoning, in the last year or so the EU has begun to change tactics and assemble the building blocks of a new China policy package. Europe is unlikely to adopt these formally as a new China strategy, and it does not need to. The diagnosis already exists: the 2019 strategy crystallised a consensus about Beijing as a systemic rival and the European Commission president has since given a clear account of the system-level challenge. The language is there and so are most of the instruments. From major legislative proposals such as the 2026 Industrial Accelerator Act (IAA) and 2026 revisions to the Cyber Security Act (CSA), the EU has made a flurry of moves in which China, though often unnamed, is the main target. The lack of fanfare is intentional; officials have characterised the approach as “say less, do more.” Some of these moves are ambitious in scope, especially if they are adopted and implemented in their most expansive form, from “Buy European” provisions to security restrictions that would lock Chinese firms out from certain sectors. They have also happened quickly. The commission’s decision to cut EU funding from energy projects using inverters from China and other high-risk states moved forward in a matter of weeks. When combined with some of the most effective tools to counter Chinese practices, such as the 2023 Foreign Subsidies Regulation (FSR) the elements of a rebalanced European approach are already taking shape. But the execution of these policies has not caught up with the analysis. Europe still largely deals with China as though it were a normal commercial actor to be lobbied, negotiated with and occasionally subjected to a trade-defence measure or subsidy investigation rather than as the systemic rival the EU says it is. Much of this is because member states, wary of retaliation and subject to lobbying from firms with strong market and supply-chain dependencies on China, have not backed a more assertive line. But part of it is that the commission itself has not put forward a fully systemic approach, instead advancing a set of distinct initiatives it has not welded into a strategically disciplined framework. Lacking an overarching strategy, these efforts remain siloed. Major gaps are present in the timetables before legislation kicks in and some at-risk industrial sectors remain exposed. What is more, whole categories of risk fall between instruments. For example, the EU lacks a tool specifically designed to address the concentrated, firm-level sourcing from China that drives Europe’s dependencies. So, while the EU’s recent policy momentum is promising, it is not yet close to taking on the spectrum of challenges that China brings. There is also a strong chance that the pattern will repeat itself, especially if some member state government are fearful of China’s response to a more coherent approach. The tasks ahead of the EU—adding new trade tools, putting defensive measures in place, closing implementation gaps and readying the Anti-Coercion Instrument (ACI)—are essential. But each addresses the same problem in a different form, and there are not enough better-tailored tools. Beijing has absorbed, adapted to or circumvented Europe’s responses when policymakers have mounted them one product and one sector at a time, each addressing the symptom it was built for while the dynamics driving it moved on to the next front. A party state is built to absorb costs that would deter a commercial actor: it can direct firms to swallow tariffs, sustain loss-making production and hold positions for strategic rather than commercial reasons, for as long as leaders judge the objective to be worth it. Each instrument that works by raising the cost of a behaviour at the margin is playing where China is strongest. And each time European policymakers discuss how to respond, they do so against a backdrop of deeper dependencies, greater industrial pressure and diminished leverage. The question is how to break the cycle. The imitation trap One answer might be to try to beat China at its own game. This great power envy could see the EU trying a “ reverse Deng ”: extracting technology from foreign firms, loosening antitrust rules, matching China’s subsidies and industrial targets, and building a more centralised and controlled economic system. Or perhaps they EU might take its cue from America and reach for large-scale tariffs or unilateral export controls. But Europe should not attempt to outmuscle its opponents in contests it is bound to lose. Beijing’s methods work because a one-party state stands behind it. Chinese policymakers can direct firms and capital, absorb losses and hold positions for purely strategic gain, and conduct the kind of extra-legal political warfare that Europe cannot. Washington’s sharpest instruments rely on a unified executive that can impose controls at speed, exploit the reach of the dollar, and draw on a deep intelligence picture fused with the authority to act on it. There are good reasons to doubt, for instance, whether Europe could extract from Chinese firms what China once extracted from European companies. Beijing is now actively restricting the outbound flow of its technology, tightening controls over personnel, intellectual property and data precisely to prevent leakage. It also has decades of experience on the other side of this exchange–engineering and politicising technology transfer—that Europe cannot match. It would keep its willingness to use investment promises and threats of withdrawal for overtly political ends. Europe can narrow some gaps by borrowing from the most effective American and Chinese tools. But it will not be able to close them because it runs counter to how the EU works. Instead, Europe has a different kind of strength altogether. It lies in the way its member states act together, and in the institutions that let them turn collective decisions into durable, legitimate rules. This is all underpinned by the world’s largest single market. That capacity is what allows Europe to embed standards in global production systems by turning access to its market into terms that others have to meet. Used deliberately, that system is a strategic instrument in its own right. The principles In some rare cases, the EU has fought on its own terms successfully by setting conditions the Chinese system struggled to satisfy. Together they point to what a more coherent strategy could look like. The clearest example is the FSR . Chinese firms often win in Europe due to advantages that are invisible on the balance sheet. This includes cheap loans from state policy banks, support shuffled between arms of the same conglomerate and inputs bought below market price. The FSR forces these into the open. Any firm bidding for a large public contract must declare the state financial support it has received, and an investigation can require it to document the whole structure behind it. For a Chinese company, that means putting on the legal record the structures Beijing prefers to keep hidden. In several cases, Chinese companies have withdrawn within weeks of an investigation being opened. The same logic also holds in the security domain. The EU addressed the threat from Chinese telecom vendors in its 5G network through criteria that firms subject to authoritarian-state intelligence laws, like Huawei and ZTE, structurally could not satisfy. It holds for reciprocity too: the International Procurement Instrument (IPI) rests on the straightforward proposition that Chinese firms should not compete freely for public contracts in the EU while European firms are systematically excluded in China . In each case the principle is the same. That is, access to the European market carries conditions, and in each case the condition is one a transparent market actor can meet but an opaque, state-directed one cannot. These cases exemplify a set of principles Europe should make explicit, defend openly and then drive through the entire framework. They are not a clever trick to keep Chinese firms out. Some Chinese firms will meet them; others will not, or could not without ceasing to be instruments of the party state. The aim is less to penalise individual transactions than to change the incentives across the market, so that operating transparently becomes the easier way to do business in Europe. The common principle is that access to Europe’s market requires a level of reciprocity, transparency and security compatibility that does not currently exist with China. Applied consistently, that principle resolves into six conditions, the Six Nos. The Six Nos No double standards : Third-country firms should receive no greater access to the European market than European firms receive in theirs. No rigged competition : Firms competing in Europe should do so on market terms, not through state subsidies or by evading standards European producers must meet. No monopolies : No single foreign supplier should be able to cut-off or weaponise a strategic supply chain. No backdoors : Products connected to European networks, or capable of receiving remote updates, must meet verifiable security standards. No black boxes : Firms operating in strategic sectors must disclose ownership, financing and state affiliations. No threat enablers : Access to the European market, supply chains or financial systems should not extend to firms supporting hostile military or coercive activity against Europe. None of these conditions are China-specific but all of them have very clear applications when it comes to the structural challenges that the Chinese system poses to Europe. In practice, the trajectory of Chinese policy breaks each one of them. Market access is becoming less reciprocal as China becomes more self-reliant. Chinese subsidies are growing larger and harder to trace, increasingly embedded in guidance funds and local-government support mechanisms. China is deliberately deepening Europe’s dependencies and converting them into leverage, as its rare-earth and magnet restrictions show. Its intelligence and data-security laws are closing the gap between the state and technology vendors. Meanwhile, transparency is shrinking, with due-diligence firms raided, the counter-espionage law expanded and commercial data increasingly restricted. And to top it off, Beijing’s support for Russia has deepened behind a widening counter-sanctions architecture. The Six Nos translate into distinct agendas for action: reciprocity rules for market access; fair-competition tools against subsidised overcapacity; diversification requirements for chokepoints; trusted-vendor standards for connected infrastructure; transparency obligations for strategic sectors; and security-compliance measures against firms enabling threats to Europe. Implemented effectively, these conditions could alter the structure of the market so that transparency, diversification and competitive neutrality become the rational choices for firms seeking access to Europe. And because these conditions can be shared with other partners, their effect grows with scale: the more economies that apply them, the harder it is for Beijing to exert pressure on any one of them. Their deeper advantage is predictability. Rather than an endless cycle of case-by-case risk assessments and ad-hoc adjustments, the Six Nos set out in advance how Europe will apply its rules, letting firms invest for the long term with confidence, and denying any single company or government the chance to pick off measures one at a time. The aim is to make the conditions of access to Europe’s market clear, durable and difficult to waive under pressure. The table below sets this out in operational form: an implementation matrix identifying, for each condition, the direction of Chinese policy, the existing European architecture, what the EU can deploy now and what it needs to build. Swarm tactics For each of these conditions, Europe should take several steps to build its architecture of rules and develop the instruments to back them up. But some of the most significant initiatives—the IAA, the CSA revisions—will take years to bite, and instruments still under consideration, from a resilience tool to a European version of US Section 301 , are at least two years away. Getting these moving matters, but in the meantime the EU needs a “good enough for now” approach that uses as many existing tools as possible with speed, legal flexibility and directed focus. One lesson of the EU’s EV tariffs on China was that a single high-profile target made it easy for China to concentrate its lobbying, so the EU spent a disproportionate amount of political capital compared with how little the measures achieved. An across-the-board tariff on China—even if it is clean, elegant and proportionate—would likely fail politically for the same reason. Instead, Europe has to exploit smallness and scale at once by using a swarm of distributed measures, with no single one decisive enough to be worth retaliating against, but enough in aggregate to achieve the effect required. This works like a military swarm, in which a single, high-value platform concentrates risk, but many smaller, expendable units spread it. It is far harder for Beijing to swat away 50 small measures, dispersed at once than one large and threatening one, and because there are so many, they can be dialled up, down or dropped without collapsing the whole. This combines certainty and uncertainty, by design. The principles are fixed and announced: any firm knows the conditions of access and knows that breaching them carries consequences. But the specific measures—which instrument, applied where and when—are deliberately unpredictable. A state actor can optimise against a single known threat, but it cannot easily pre-position against many smaller ones whose sequence and targets it cannot read in advance. Europe should be predictable about its terms and unpredictable about its tactics; the first to give compliant firms confidence, the second to keep Beijing off-balance. As the tables above show, a swarm of this kind can be built, now, from instruments that are already at the EU’s disposal. The purpose is to deploy them together, rather than one at a time, concentrated on the sectors that matter most and applied under the Six Nos, so that what can look like a scattered set of moves instead translates into the consistent application of clear principles. For example, safeguards on surging imports, a wider run of subsidy investigations, procurement instruments deployed across sectors, product-safety and security audits pursued systematically and national subsidy criteria recalibrated. None is decisive alone but deployed at once under a common rationale, they come from too many directions for Beijing to fend off. All the while, they are defensible as rules-based enforcement rather than a trade war by other means. Right now, however, the European “swarm” is still a minor enough set of irritants that China can swat them away. The goal is to achieve cumulative effects that add up to a quantified sense of “enough”. Assessing what that means is harder with 50 small measures than 2 large ones, but ballpark figures are politically important. That is, the commission needs to be able to credibly tell member states that holding the line against Chinese coercion will keep key industrial sectors alive and close major vulnerabilities. The swarm should therefore be judged by results in a few critical areas: whether import surges slow, exposed firms survive long enough for alternative capacity to come online, single-country dependencies fall, price gaps narrow, and Beijing faces too many legally grounded measures to defeat through pressure on any one capital or company. Making it a reality Coalitions within Europe Internal politics Implementing these principles—whether by a swarm of measures now, or with new and improved architecture in the medium term—will require a stronger political consensus in Europe than there has been in recent years. Yet that consensus is now closer than ever. Within the EU, the diagnosis is largely shared . France, once a dirigiste outlier, now leads a cluster of member states arguing that Europe cannot preserve market liberalism against a state-capitalist competitor of China’s scale without attaching conditions to access its market. Even states with liberal, open-trade instincts have moved this way, concluding that openness without reciprocity, transparency and security is no longer sustainable. Even on climate politics, there is a growing recognition that a green transition built on unsafe products and monopolistic supply chains that Beijing can cut at will is not a secure transition, and that a decarbonisation that hollows out European industry will lose the domestic coalition that underpins it. Some differences in exposure remain. European exporters with stakes in the Chinese market have greater fears of retaliation, while frontline states have greater concern about the wartime vulnerability of connected infrastructure. But the old maps of hawks and doves, a central European bloc versus western Europe, south versus north have faded. Among the supposed outliers, Hungary is likely less of a spoiler state after its election, while Spain courts Chinese investment but has not worked against the core direction of EU policy. Germany remains the hinge. A few of its large firms with concentrated stakes in China have dominated the debate about acceptable risk, even as much of the rest of German industry is becoming more and more exposed to Chinese competition at home and abroad. The result is that constituencies once reflexively opposed to trade-defence measures have begun to move. Germany’s VDMA, the machinery and plant-engineering association, is traditionally one of the country’s most committed free-trade voices, whose export-oriented members long resisted anything that might provoke Chinese retaliation. Now, it argues openly that China is not competing fairly and that policymakers must respond, calling for tariffs, local-content rules and the enforcement of European standards on Chinese imports. This leaves a shrinking number of firms whose commercial interests are no longer a proxy for the wider German economy, and which continue to resist the defence of the market that the rest of the economy depends on. They oppose limits on Chinese imports, including imports of their own China-made goods into Europe; oppose restrictions on insecure technologies and components they are building into their products and plants; and oppose any measures to address the supply-chain concentrations they rely on and have doubled down on. In the medium term, the Six Nos would tilt the balance of incentives, making it harder for these firms to keep internalising the gains of their China exposure while externalising its costs—the dependency, the security risk, the displaced competition—onto the wider economy. Conditions that bite on imports from Chinese production bases, on insecure technology built into European systems and on concentrated sourcing would make them carry the risk they create rather than pass it on. In the short term, however, the choice falls on Berlin to decide whether the European economy is held hostage to a shrinking number of firms whose interests have become increasingly detached from it. Underlying all these differences is a shared European interest. Even as the immediate costs of targeted Chinese retaliation fall unevenly, no member state can insulate itself from a major supply-chain rupture or a disorderly escalation with Beijing. That is the main political case for the swarm: many sub-threshold measures, applied under broad principles and backed by pre-agreed support, are easier to sustain than one large showdown in which every capital must decide whether it can bear the next act of retaliation. Among European countries, the coalition for maximal confrontation does not exist, but the political conditions for disciplined, cumulative action increasingly do. Forging coalitions Despite political positions now aligning more closely, full EU agreement and implementation will still take time the situation does not allow. To ensure the most impact as soon as possible, tighter clusters of like-minded countries should step in to bridge the gap. Smaller groups of willing states moving ahead of full consensus is increasingly accepted—and encouraged at senior levels—across EU policy on defence, competitiveness and economic security; the China agenda should be no exception. A vanguard group could be ready to take faster collective action on China-related national-security risks, aligned with but ahead of the EU timetable. Frontline states that have weathered Russian hybrid operations and are at heightened risk of attack have strong grounds for addressing the dangers of technology exposure to Moscow’s strategic partner . They can do so through a framework of security requirements covering critical communications, energy, and port and logistics infrastructure, connected vehicles, and AI and data infrastructure. Most security-exposed states have already begun to move in this direction, though unevenly , and at a slower pace than their own threat assessments imply. To align their responses an informal coalition, a Nordic-Baltic-Polish grouping for instance, should be agreed in close coordination with the EU, NATO, and key external partners that can cooperate on threat assessments. The heightened threat level gives these countries a strong incentive and justification for acting faster while the grouping provides more cover against retaliation than going it alone. It would also set a precedent for other European states and create pressure in the European Council to keep the EU timetable from lagging too far behind. Another area in which small coalitions can have a big effect is national-level subsidies. The impact on Chinese EV sales from the French ecological bonus criteria , which incorporated carbon-footprint scoring across the supply chain, is a good example. Its introduction produced a dramatic collapse in Chinese firms’ share of the French market, as their coal-heavy supply chains and shipping distances pushed them above the carbon threshold. The three leading China-made models’ share of French EV sales fell from a peak of 32% to 4% in the space of a few months after the new eligibility rules took effect. Subsidy rules could play a significant role elsewhere too, including in agricultural machinery, heat pumps, e-bikes and solar inverters. A few of the major member states with subsidy schemes in these areas, that were willing to use carbon, cyber-security or resilience criteria, would already span much of the European market. If a handful of major states—Germany, France, the Netherlands, Italy, and Poland—attached similar criteria to their subsidy schemes, this would cover most European demand. A producer would then face a clear choice to meet the standard or lose access to the subsidised market. Coalitions beyond Europe Europe’s China strategy can also achieve scale through closer coordination with external partners. Building supply chains independent of China requires a market large enough to support them. Below a certain threshold, alternative producers cannot reach viable scale and cost, and diversification stays permanently dependent on subsidies. No single market, the EU’s included, reliably clears that threshold alone, and only a handful of partners have markets large enough to matter. Aligning the EU with a few other major economies, primarily with the UK and some Asian countries, for example, would assemble combined demand on a scale that makes non-Chinese capacity commercially viable, and does so to the mutual advantage of each side’s firms. Coordination among a few large markets is worth far more than wider agreement among many small ones. European have invested heavily in the China coordination agenda with the US. But under the Trump administration, it is becoming increasingly clear this will achieve limited gains for the EU beyond moves that the US makes on its own , such as exclusions of Chinese companies in sectors where European firms compete. It is possible that this year’s EU-US agreement on critical raw materials (CRMs) could translate into more concerted action, and then be replicated in other areas such as legacy semiconductors, but even progress on an issue as urgent as CRMs has been far slower than necessary and US willingness to embark on larger-scale coordination to build transatlantic scale across a wider range of sectors has been limited, despite the implications that the hit to key industries in Europe such as chemicals and machine tools would have for its own supply chains. Equally, while EU trade diversification efforts have accelerated, including a new set of major free-trade deals, this has not translated into a club-building effort on China policies as such. Rather, key partners’ decisions in areas such as consumer subsidies, tariffs and cyber rules are being made in isolation from each other. Achieving greater openness of new markets will have limited effect if European firms find themselves dealing with exactly the same Chinese economic practices in these markets too. The UK is second only to the US as a market for European firms, but in some areas runs a weaker line on Chinese overcapacity and subsidies than the EU, to the detriment of the European companies displaced there. Already apparent in EVs , this gap is likely to widen in other sectors as EU efforts step up. Together, the EU and the UK could form a strong nucleus for a common approach, from security in connected sectors to building alternative non-Chinese supply chains where China currently dominates. Bespoke partnerships with Japan, Korea and India, going beyond the existing free-trade agreements, make sense too. None of this would require identical policies, given the different tools and legal systems, nor would they require any public “countering China” rhetoric. But rough convergence on as many principles as possible—defensive trade and anti-subsidy measures, procurement and subsidy criteria, cyber rules in connected sectors, and joint supply-chain build-out—would have a significant impact. The adequacy framework of the EU’s General Data Protection Regulation is a useful model for this, under which countries with compatible protections gain easier access to European data flows. Extended more broadly, partners meeting European standards on reciprocity, transparency, diversification and security would achieve deeper economic integration graduated to their degree of alignment: preferential access under the IAA and other support schemes, fast-tracked conformity and security clearances, eligibility for joint procurement and supply-chain partnerships, and priority in technology cooperation. The benefits would flow through to these countries’ firms, which would clear European market-access conditions as by default, while those that do not align would face case-by-case scrutiny. Many of these conversations, with London, Tokyo, and others, are already under way. It is a reminder that the political process of building a framework to deal with China is not only about saying no to opacity, subsidy and coercion but yes to the partners and firms that meet its terms: transparency, fair competition and security. An industrial policy calibrated to the threat The defensive elements of the Six Nos framework mostly buy space. Several of them depend on Europe having something to switch to: “no monopolies” can exclude a dominant supplier only where an alternative exists, and “no backdoors” can bar an insecure vendor only where a trusted one can take its place. Where that capacity has disappeared, the framework manages dependence rather than moving beyond it. Europe does not lack industrial-policy frameworks or competitiveness priority lists, from the Net Zero Industry Act and Critical Raw Materials Act (CRMA) to the Chips Act, the IAA, and the wider response to the Draghi report. But it lacks an allocation of funding driven by a clear reading of the Chinese challenge and where it is heading—which sectors Beijing is targeting next, where it is directing its industrial plans and subsidies, and where the next phase of competition will be decided. China is a rapidly moving target and Europe’s assessment needs to not just keep up but to be a step ahead. The problem is that this reading does not currently exist in any one place. What the EU knows about Chinese industrial capability is scattered across the commission, national agencies, intelligence services and outside analysts, and rarely pulled together into a forward assessment that can drive decisions. Assembling one—pooling what member states and EU bodies know about where Chinese capability is concentrated, where subsidies and industrial plans are pointed, and how fast the frontier is moving—is the precondition for the rest. This should tell Europe which dependencies to price, which sectors to defend, where to build and where Beijing has already moved on. Without it, funding allocation risks defaulting to whoever lobbies hardest or to the crisis most recently in the headlines. This reading should also determine how Europe builds: where defensive measures will suffice, where Europe’s lost capacity must be rebuilt, where viable positions need preventive defence, and where Europe needs to bolster its forward position. And finally, this should indicate what it spends. With resources scarce, concentration on the decisive sectors matters more than breadth. Beyond industrial policy, Europe’s wider competitiveness agenda—the Draghi and Letta reports, the completion of the single market, the 2025 Competitiveness Compass roadmap—has been driven mostly by internal assessments of productivity gaps, fragmentation and scale, with China treated as one factor among many. The reading of where Chinese competition is heading should do more to set the priorities of that agenda, including where to merge into bigger and more competitive companies, which national barriers inside the single market should be removed most urgently, and where and when to direct investment. Pursued without that lens, the agenda risks spreading scarce effort too thinly and defending ground the contest has already moved past. Facing up to China For all this to work, European governments must finally be ready to stand up for themselves against Beijing—and ready to bear the consequences. Former commission president Jean-Claude Juncker once captured the politician’s dilemma: “we all know what to do, but we don’t know how to get re-elected once we have done it.” On China, the first half is largely true; European leaders broadly know what their policy requires. The difficulty is the second half. That is, how can they sustain public support when Beijing inflicts further harm in response. Part of the problem is that Europe still pursues goals that are no longer credible. Leaders go into meetings with requests, demands and warnings they cannot plausibly believe will change Chinese behaviour. Exhorting Xi Jinping to rebalance the Chinese economy or reconsider his alignment with Russia was once a defensible statement of position; repeated without consequence, it has simply depleted European credibility. Beijing’s read is now that its relationship with Russia has cost it little in Europe, that Europe is not serious about defending its economy, and that it can slow and contain whatever measures are under way. Europe also still pursues legacy goals from an earlier era—above all the pursuit of getting China to “open up”. Broad market access mattered when China was growing fast and access meant exports; with weak demand, eroding foreign market share, a narrowing European technology edge, and risks of leakage where it survives, the case for spending political capital on this has largely gone. Reviving the Comprehensive Agreement on Investment with China is not worth meaningful attention. Europe’s best path is to treat China’s posture—the securitisation of its economy and its alignment with Russia—as locked in. As such, Europeans should understand bilateral engagements primarily as a means of buying the political space to make the adjustments Europe needs, including reducing dependencies that expose it to coercion, putting trade on a footing compatible with a thriving industrial base and addressing the vulnerabilities created by China’s backing for threats to European security. Persuading Beijing to change course is a lost cause. What Europeans should focus on now is preventing coercion while Europe changes the terms of the relationship. This is not an argument for disengagement. If anything, a firmer European posture requires a higher intensity of bilateral exchanges, not least to manage frictions, explore negotiating avenues and prevent crises. Europe should therefore pair its new approach with investment in the machinery of managing the relationship: more regular and more candid official channels, crisis-communication mechanisms for when tensions are high and structured negotiation tracks on the issues where bargaining is possible. A secondary goal is to widen that narrow negotiating space. Each EU instrument rests on its own legal basis, so it cannot easily be turned up, turned down or traded against one another; and because the measures currently in place barely counter the Chinese threat, giving any of them up in a negotiation is a major risk. The swarm answers both constraints. A package deliberately built with more measures than it strictly needs can afford to give ground in one area without falling short overall. If China can replicate or exceed the effects of European measures through binding commitments of its own, or make lateral offers of greater value, the EU should be ready to act. It can do so by pulling back specific actions and reducing the intensity of enforcement in areas where China makes verifiable progress. The best outcome is to trade a withdrawal for Chinese concessions, and an acceptable one is to trade it for a reduction in Chinese retaliation while keeping the rest of the framework intact. At worst, Beijing could try to coerce Europe into abandoning the defence of its economy and security altogether. But even that would carry an advantage as coercion on that scale would unlock a different set of opportunities for Europe to act. Never waste a crisis The EU needs to be ready to move decisively when conditions allow. European policymakers tend to see Chinese coercion as a problem to be managed entirely defensively: avoiding the triggers, absorbing the consequences and finding off-ramps that restore the previous status quo. The opposite approach is required. Moments when Chinese behaviour creates the political conditions for a decisive European response need to be treated as opportunities the EU can capitalise on. Beijing’s own approach is characterised by a “ reactive assertiveness ”, in which it waits for (or engineers) provocations, then responds with far more assertive measures than the trigger warranted. This way, it can frame the response as defensive reaction rather than initiated escalation. The Scarborough Shoal stand off in 2012, the 2010 Senkaku rare-earth restrictions, the 2020 Australian coordinated trade measures, the 2017 THAAD response against South Korea and the 2021–22 Lithuanian case, for example, all followed this pattern. China has also used these moments to build new instruments of economic statecraft such as its export-control law framework, Unreliable Entity List, anti-foreign-sanctions law and blocking regulations. All of these emerged from or were developed in response to specific incidents but with a structural objective: each moment of confrontation produced lasting capacity rather than just a one-off effect. While Europe should not seek to engineer crises of its own, it should be ready to adopt a version of strategic ratcheting using coercive episodes to deploy countermeasures and to consolidate instruments that would otherwise face political resistance. A coercive episode can do three things that ordinary conditions cannot. First, it opens a political window: measures that cautious member states and firms would block in normal times become passable when the cost of opposing them has just been demonstrated by Beijing. Second, it provides a legal trigger: an episode crossing the threshold for activating the ACI unlocks powers that are otherwise unavailable. And third, it provides justification: the first use of a contested instrument is always the hardest, but an instrument first deployed as a defensive response to documented coercion is normalised and faces far less resistance the second and third time. Major episodes of Chinese pressure create the political permission and momentum to use or enact an instrument that is designed, ready and waiting. This is why the EU needs to be prepared. Such a window will open unpredictably and close quickly, so the EU will need measures drafted in advance. Each episode, handled this way, leaves behind something that has entered the permanent repertoire. This could be a tool, a precedent or an expectation it will be used again. The ACI itself is the sharpest illustration of why this inverts the pressure. It is the most flexible tool Europe has: once activated, it places measures that currently sit on separate legal bases under a single one, which is what allows them to be scaled up, dialled back or traded off as a package rather than defended one by one. That is precisely the flexibility Europe otherwise lacks, and it is unlocked only by a Chinese coercive act serious enough to cross the threshold. The more forcefully Beijing coerces, the more completely it hands Europe the instrument that makes the entire strategy easier to run. Large-scale coercion is therefore not just a risk Europe must weather but a trap for China, as the response it provokes is more capable, more flexible and more durable than anything Europe could put in place without it. The institutional preparation for this to happen requires pre-emptive work inside the commission: from pre-drafted countermeasure packages that can be deployed within days of ACI activation and quietly road-tested with member states, to pre-positioned coalition agreements with external partners on response to specific Chinese actions. It also needs communication strategies ready to go that frame the European response as the application of established principles. Large-scale Chinese retaliation against any serious European action is far from a certainty. Beijing faces vulnerabilities that mean there are risks attached to a major economic confrontation with its most important market. For all its surface bullishness, China is experiencing fragile growth, weak domestic consumption, fiscal stress, high debt and a stagnant property market. It is still conceivable that more serious European moves could lead to bargaining rather than debilitating levels of coercion given the risk that large-scale coercion will blow back against China. It is one thing for Beijing to mount a full-scale response when facing 145% across-the-board tariffs, another when the EU is deploying a dispersed set of low-key measures, not one of which is a clear cause for large-scale retaliation. But the package this paper proposes is designed to absorb the shock either way. It is distributed enough to be difficult to counter comprehensively; over-determined enough that some measures can be withdrawn in negotiation; embedded in coalitions broad enough to share the political costs; robust enough to be worth the fight; and defensible enough to European publics that it can command support. It should also be ready to go the minute Chinese coercion crosses the threshold. The intended endpoint with China should be a stable settlement rather than a lasting struggle. Europe’s objectives are entirely consistent with a thriving Chinese economy, continued technological advances and leading firms in key industries. The measures proposed do not corner or contain Beijing, and there is no inherent reason why a negotiated equilibrium on different terms should be impossible. But this requires Europe to demonstrate credibility: that a new equilibrium is the best outcome that Beijing can achieve, because Europe is willing to defend its industry, its security and the viability of its model as if they are core interests. Saying yes to no The “no” Europe needs is not the one Beijing gave all those years ago. China’s “no” was the beginning of its path into self-reliance. Europe’s is the opposite: a no said to ensure it can keep its market open. European openness cannot survive if it extends to those who exploit it, taking access while denying their own, or use it to dominate the markets they enter. Drawing that line is the price for Europe of keeping its principles. The decisions facing European policymakers are now different in kind from those of recent years. By now, policymakers widely recognise that China poses a systemic challenge to European economic power. Whether Europe can act on it, however, is less clear. The instruments exist or can readily be built, the principles are defensible and the coalitions can be assembled. So far, the EU has reached for these tools defensively, one sector at a time, often a step behind and never organised by a common principle. What has been missing is the resolve to use them together as a system and on Europe’s own terms. This course will not be pain-free. It will raise prices in some sectors, expose exporters to retaliation and affect member states unevenly. But those costs are smaller and more bearable than the alternative: a slide into dependence that leaves Europe absorbing the same shocks from a weaker position, at a price it is already paying, with its choices narrowing and its model under threat. China decided it could say no. The question now is whether Europe can. About the author Andrew Small is the director of the Asia programme at the European Council on Foreign Relations. He is the author of “The Rupture”, also titled “No Limits”, about the transformation of European and American policy toward China, and was the first China fellow at IDEA, the advisory hub that reports to the European Commission president Acknowledgments The author would like to thank his colleagues on the Asia team for their excellent research support, Portia Kentish for her fantastic editorial support and Janka Oertel for all her invaluable inputs. The paper also drew from discussions at workshops held in Berlin and Brussels in February and May 2026, as well as exchanges with a wide range of European officials [1] ECFR and Rhodium Group input paper “Pricing in the China downside risks – counting the costs of dependency, dominance, and limited de-risking”, June, 2026 Source

items.title: Imaan, Hadi seek early hearing of appeal against IHC decision in social media posts case
items.description: ISLAMABAD: Lawyers Imaan Zainab Mazari-Hazir and Hadi Ali Chattha filed a petition in the Supreme Court (SC) on Wednesday, seeking an early hearing of their challenge to a Feb 19 Islamabad High Court (IHC) decision in the controversial social media posts case. On Jan 24, an Islamabad sessions court sentenced Imaan and Hadi to a total of 17 years in jail on multiple charges under the Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act (Peca). In February, the IHC had denied the lawyers’ plea for suspension of their 17-year sentences under Peca. The controversy at the centre of the case stems from a complaint filed under Peca on August 12, 2025, by the NCCIA Islamabad assistant director (investigating officer) before the Cybercrime Reporting Centre, FIA. The complaint accused Imaan of disseminating and “propagating narratives that align with hostile terrorist groups and proscribed organisations”, while her husband was implicated for reposting some of her posts. Filed through senior counsel Faisal Siddiqi, the application on Wednesday sought the fixation of an earlier appeal challenging the sentence under Article 185(3) of the Constitution and requested a hearing next week. Although the high court issued notices to the respondents on the application, it did not suspend their sentences. “It is the settled policy of the Supreme Court that criminal matters were given priority, especially when the matters pertain to bail or suspension of sentence,” the new application said. It also highlighted Section 7 of the SC (Practice and Procedure) Act 2023, which states that any application pleading urgency should be fixed for hearing within a period of 14 days. Imaan and Hadi have been in jail since their arrest in January in a case registered against the two for protesting outside the IHC and allegedly manhandling the IHC Bar Association (IHCBA) president. While the arrest prompted criticism by rights bodies, politicians, and journalists, who stressed the couple’s right to a fair trial, a sessions court sentenced them to 17 years in prison in the social media posts case just a day after the development. The couple had challenged their conviction by filing separate appeals in the IHC on February 7. On April 30, they had moved another appeal in the SC, seeking an early hearing of their pleas against their conviction.

items.title: Laprophan finalise sa première acquisition en Europe et prend le contrôle du suisse Rivopharm
items.description: RÉVÉLATION. Le groupe pharmaceutique marocain Laprophan a finalisé, ce 17 juin, l’acquisition d’une participation majoritaire dans Rivopharm, société suisse spécialisée dans les médicaments de spécialité et les solutions pharmaceutiques à forte valeur ajoutée. Dr Farid Bennis la qualifie de "première historique : le médicament marocain est reconnu à l’international et en Suisse, pays de l’industrie pharmaceutique mondiale". The post Laprophan finalise sa première acquisition en Europe et prend le contrôle du suisse Rivopharm appeared first on Médias24 - Numéro un de l'information économique marocaine .

items.title: SOTRACO : Neuf machinistes et trois étudiants déférés à la Maison d’arrêt et de correction de Bobo-Dioulasso
items.description: Neuf machinistes de la Société de transport en commun (SOTRACO) et trois étudiants ont été déférés à la Maison d'arrêt et de correction de Bobo-Dioulasso (MACB). L'information a été rapportée par le quotidien d'État Sidwaya, le mardi 16 juin 2026. Au total, douze personnes ont été présentées devant le parquet et placées en détention à la MACB : neuf machinistes, employés de la SOTRACO, et trois étudiants. Selon Sidwaya, les prévenus sont poursuivis pour fraudes répétées au préjudice de la société de transport. Les trois étudiants, quant à eux, ont été arrêtés pour falsification de cartes d'abonnement. D'après Sidwaya, ils ont en mis en place un système de contrefaçon des cartes d'abonnement destinées aux élèves et étudiants, dans le but de s'enrichir illicitement. D'après les sources de Kantigui, tous les présumés délinquants sont actuellement détenus à la MACB, dans l'attente de la suite de la procédure judiciaire. Lire aussi : SOTRACO : Plusieurs machinistes licenciés pour faute grave Minute.bf

items.description: L'IA est partout autour de nous, pour le meilleur, sans doute, mais aussi pour le pire. En la matière, son utilisation dans la guerre est peut-être celle qui pose le plus de questions. Gaza, Ukraine, Iran... l'IA facilite l'analyse de données et l'identification des cibles. Va-t-elle finir par échapper au contrôle de l'humain ? Vit-on les prémices d'une guerre sans humains ? Quid des interrogations ethniques, morales et juridiques ?
